skill.name: modal
skill.description: Guides agents through using Modal for serverless Python workloads, including GPU jobs, batch processing, web endpoints, secrets, volumes, and scheduled tasks.
skill.summary: Deploy and operate serverless Python workloads on Modal with GPUs, scaling, secrets, volumes, APIs, and scheduled jobs.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but AI review found they are mainly documentation examples for Modal rather than hidden executable skill code. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found. The skill still warrants a medium risk level because it teaches cloud command execution, secret handling, network endpoints, and persistent file operations.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Modal CLI usage, image build commands, subprocess execution, and a web server example using shell=True. This is legitimate Modal guidance, but it can become unsafe if an agent inserts untrusted input into commands or runs commands without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct command execution examples are present, including subprocess and shell=True. The context is normal Modal documentation, so the issue is capability risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Secrets and Environment Variable Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches using Modal secrets, dotenv files, API keys, and cloud credentials through environment variables. This is expected for deployment workflows, but agents must avoid printing, hardcoding, or copying secret values into generated code or logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines clearly access secrets and environment variables. The usage is framed as secure Modal secret management, not exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Network Services and External API Access
medium_findings.description: The skill includes web endpoint patterns and examples that call external APIs with authorization headers. These are core Modal capabilities, but they can expose services or move data outside the local project if used without access controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network and endpoint examples are explicit, including protected endpoint and external request patterns. The examples are generic and do not show malicious destinations.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Storage and Local Credential File Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill explains writing to Modal Volumes, storing Modal tokens in a home directory file, and adding a local .aws directory into an image. These are legitimate operations, but local credential copying and persistent writes need careful review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file and credential locations are directly documented. The guidance supports normal Modal workflows, but copying credential directories into images is sensitive.

low_findings.title: False Positive: PyTorch eval and Modal spawn
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged eval and spawn keywords, but the reviewed context shows self.model.eval() for PyTorch inference and process_job.spawn(data) for Modal background execution. These are not JavaScript eval or OS process spawning in this context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding lines confirm framework method calls, not dynamic code evaluation or direct process creation. This is a high-confidence false positive.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords
low_findings.description: Several weak-cryptography findings appear to come from generic words such as API documentation, GPU model names, or storage descriptions. The reviewed lines do not show hashing, encryption, or insecure cryptographic algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation prose about APIs, H100/H200 GPUs, and Modal Volumes. No cryptographic implementation is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: A web server example starts a process with shell=True. This pattern should not be combined with untrusted input and should be reviewed before reuse.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact dangerous subprocess pattern is present. It is a static documentation example, not hidden executable skill code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Image Build Commands with External Source Fetching
dangerous_patterns.description: The image example runs a command that clones a remote repository during image construction. Agents should pin sources and avoid copying unreviewed remote code into deployments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The build command and external URL are visible in the example. The command is illustrative, so risk depends on how users adapt it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secrets Loaded from dotenv and Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The references show secrets loaded from dotenv files and read from environment variables. This is a normal Modal pattern but sensitive values must remain out of prompts, logs, and generated examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly demonstrate secret loading and environment reads. The context is secure secret injection rather than credential theft.

content.value_statement: Cloud Python deployment often requires GPUs, autoscaling, secrets, and scheduled jobs. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Modal patterns for building and operating those workloads.
actual_capabilities: Explains Modal setup, authentication, and basic app execution.
Guides creation of Modal functions, classes, images, and dependencies.
Documents GPU selection, resource limits, autoscaling, and parallel execution.
Covers Modal Secrets, Volumes, scheduled jobs, and persistent storage patterns.
Shows how to deploy web endpoints, FastAPI handlers, and background jobs.
Provides scientific computing examples for inference, training, and batch processing.
limitations: Does not authenticate to Modal or create cloud resources by itself.
Examples may require current Modal package behavior and account permissions.
Cost, quota, and GPU availability depend on the user Modal account.
Some examples are templates and need security review before production use.

use_cases.title: Serve an ML Model
use_cases.description: Package model dependencies, request a GPU, and expose inference through a Modal endpoint.

use_cases.title: Run Parallel Batch Jobs
use_cases.description: Convert local Python data processing into Modal functions that map work across containers.

use_cases.title: Operate Scheduled Cloud Tasks
use_cases.description: Create cron or periodic jobs that use secrets, volumes, resource limits, and monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Create a First Modal App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Modal skill to help me create a minimal Python Modal app, explain the required authentication, and list the command I should review before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy GPU Inference
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Modal skill to design a GPU inference service for my Python model. Include image dependencies, GPU choice, endpoint shape, and secret handling.

prompt_templates.title: Scale Batch Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Modal skill to convert my local batch processing workflow into parallel Modal functions with sensible CPU, memory, timeout, and volume settings.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Modal Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Modal skill to review my planned Modal deployment for security, cost, scaling, secrets, persistent storage, and operational risks before I deploy it.

output_examples.input: I need to serve a text classification model on a GPU.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a Modal app structure, a pinned dependency image, an L40S or A10 GPU choice, a secure secret plan, and deployment checks.

output_examples.input: I have 10,000 files to process with pandas.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines a mapped Modal function, volume or object storage options, resource limits, timeout settings, and result collection guidance.

output_examples.input: I want a nightly data sync job that uses an API key.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a scheduled Modal function, Modal Secret usage, idempotent writes, monitoring steps, and failure handling.

best_practices: Ask for confirmation before running Modal CLI commands or deploying cloud resources.
Use Modal Secrets for credentials and keep secret values out of prompts and logs.
Pin dependencies, set resource limits, and review cost impact before production deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode API keys, tokens, or cloud credentials in generated examples.
Do not pass untrusted input into shell commands, subprocess calls, or image build commands.
Do not deploy public endpoints without authentication, rate limits, and data handling review.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents design and explain Modal apps for serverless Python, GPUs, batch jobs, endpoints, secrets, volumes, and schedules.

faq.question: Does it run Modal commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users should approve commands before authentication, deployment, or cloud resource creation.

faq.question: Can it help with GPU workloads?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents GPU types, multi-GPU requests, CUDA image patterns, and common inference or training workflows.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use Modal Secrets or approved environment variables. Never place real secret values in prompts, source code, logs, or marketplace examples.

faq.question: Is it safe for production deployments?
faq.answer: It can support production planning, but generated deployments still need review for commands, access control, costs, quotas, and data movement.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it covers command execution, network endpoints, credentials, and persistent storage. These capabilities require careful use.
